questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following has pleaded on the side of the Indian National Army officers in their Red Fort trial?
questions.questionText: Who among the following has pleaded on the side of the Indian National Army officers in their Red Fort trial?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The Red Fort trials, also known as the **INA trials**, involved court-martial proceedings against officers of the Indian National Army (INA) who were charged with various offenses including treason, torture, murder, and abetment to murder during World War II.
The first trial took place between November and December **1945**. In this trial, which included defendants Shah Nawaz Khan, Gurubaksh Singh Dhillon, and Prem Sahgal, the defense was led by **Sir Tej Bahadur Sapru** (Sir T.B. Sapru) along with Lt. Col. Horilal Varma, who represented the accused officers.
**

Important Tips **Sir T.B. Sapru**:
- Tej Bahadur Sapru (1875
- **1949**) was an Indian freedom fighter, lawyer, and politician.
He was a key figure in India's struggle for independence, helping draft the Indian Constitution. He was the leader of the Liberal party in British-ruled India.
- He was knighted in **1922** and after receiving knighthood he was known as sir Tej Bahadur Sapru.
- He held positions in various legislative councils, including the Legislative Council of the United Provinces and the Imperial Legislative Council, where he served as the law member of the Viceroy's Council.
- Sapru mediated between different factions, playing a key role in facilitating the Gandhi-Irwin Pact and resolving the dispute between Mahatma Gandhi and Dr. Ambedkar regarding separate electorates for the Depressed Classes, leading to the Poona Pact.
- He became a member of the Privy Council in **1934** and represented the Liberal Party in the Round Table Conferences in London.
- Sapru supported India's participation in the Second World War without prior consultation with Indians, a stance that differed from most Indian political activists at the time.
- As a lawyer, he handled significant cases such as the Meerut Conspiracy Case and defended rebel soldiers of the Indian National Army.
- Sapru's contributions extended to the Nehru Committee Report of **1928**, and although his health prevented him from serving in the Constituent Assembly after independence, his legal expertise continued to influence constitutional reforms.
- He established the Indian Council of World Affairs in **1943** and served as its president until **1949**.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: After, the arrest of Gandhiji during salt Satyagraha, who took his place as the leader of the movement?
questions.questionText: After, the arrest of Gandhiji during salt Satyagraha, who took his place as the leader of the movement?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. In May **1930**, Mahatma Gandhi chose **Tyabji**, who was seventy-six years old, to take over as the leader of the **Salt Satyagraha** following Gandhi's own arrest.
Shortly after assuming leadership, Tyabji was also arrested and detained by the British Indian Government. With admiration, Gandhi and his colleagues affectionately referred to Tyabji as the "Grand Old Man of Gujarat."

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The Muslim League Annual Session which gave effect to Jinnah's Two Nation Theory was held in:
questions.questionText: The Muslim League Annual Session which gave effect to Jinnah's Two Nation Theory was held in:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The **Lahore Resolution** was passed by the All India Muslim League at its annual session in **Lahore** on March 24, **1940**. The resolution called for the creation of a separate Muslim state in the north-west and north-east of India.
It was presented by Muhammad Ali Jinnah, who was the leader of the All India Muslim League. The Lahore Session of the Muslim League was held in **1940**, and it was the first time that the **Two-Nation Theory** was put forward.
The theory was based on the idea that Hindus and Muslims were two separate nations, and that they could not live together in a united India.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who of the following was the President of Indian National Congress for consecutive six years?
questions.questionText: Who of the following was the President of Indian National Congress for consecutive six years?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Maulana Abul Kalam Azad** was the president of the Indian National Congress for six consecutive years from **1940** to **1946**. He was also the youngest person to become the president of the Indian National Congress at the age of 35 in **1923**. He was a prominent leader of the Indian independence movement and a scholar of Islam.
He also served as the first Minister of Education in independent India. He was the first Minister of Education in the Indian government and established the University Grants Commission (UGC) in **1953**. He was also awarded the Bharat Ratna, India's highest civilian honour, posthumously in **1992**.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The National Leader who was elected President (Speaker) of the Central Legislative Assembly in 1925 was:
questions.questionText: The National Leader who was elected President (Speaker) of the Central Legislative Assembly in 1925 was:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Vithalbhai Patel**, an Indian legislator and political leader, was elected as the **President (Speaker)** of the **Central Legislative Assembly** in **1925**. He co-founded the Swaraj Party with leaders like Chittaranjan Das and Motilal Nehru.
Vithalbhai Patel played an influential role in the Indian freedom struggle. He was known for his witty speeches and efforts to challenge British rule through legislative means.
He introduced bills and amendments on various issues, including education and medical practice. As the President of the Assembly, he established important practices and procedures for its functioning.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: After the Poona Pact of 1932, Harijan Sewak Sangh was established. Its President was:
questions.questionText: After the Poona Pact of 1932, Harijan Sewak Sangh was established. Its President was:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. Anti Untouchability League founded by Mahatma Gandhi on 30 September **1932** was later renamed **Harijan Sevak Sangh**.
The aim of the Harijan Sevak Sangh was to uplift the status of the Depressed Classes, also known as the Scheduled Castes. The first president of the Harijan Sevak Sangh was **Ghanshyam Das Birla**, a prominent industrialist and philanthropist.
Amritlal Takkar was its first secretary.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: In which of the following session of Indian National Congress, Subhash Chandra Bose was elected as President for the second time?
questions.questionText: In which of the following session of Indian National Congress, Subhash Chandra Bose was elected as President for the second time?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. In the **1939**, **Tripuri** (near Jabalpur in present Madhya Pradesh) Session, **Subhash Chandra Bose** was elected as President of Indian National Congress for the second time.
He defeated Pattabhi Sitaramayya.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following had moved the Non-cooperation resolution in the Nagpur session of the Indian National Congress in 1920?
questions.questionText: Who among the following had moved the Non-cooperation resolution in the Nagpur session of the Indian National Congress in 1920?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The Non-Cooperation resolution was moved by **Chittaranjan Das (C.R. Das)** during the **Nagpur session of the Indian National Congress in 1920**. This session marked a significant shift in the Congress's approach to the freedom struggle, as it decided to pursue Swaraj (self-government) through non-cooperation with the British authorities, boycotting foreign goods, schools, and institutions.
This approach was intended to be a peaceful and legitimate means of achieving independence. **

Important Tips **1920 INC Annual Session:** The Indian National Congress (INC) held its annual session in December **1920** in Nagpur, Maharashtra, with C. Vijayaraghavachariar presiding.
This pivotal session saw the adoption of the main resolution on Non-Cooperation, which advocated for:
- Boycott of Foreign Goods, Schools, and Courts: The resolution urged Indians to boycott British goods, institutions, and the legal system.
- Surrender of Titles: Indians were encouraged to give up honorary titles bestowed by the British government as a sign of non-cooperation.
- Harmony Between Muslims and Hindus: The session emphasized promoting unity and understanding between the two major communities.
- Congress Working Committee: The formation of a 15-member committee to guide the Congress's activities was a significant outcome.
- Shift in Approach: The session marked a shift from constitutional means to achieving Swaraj (self-government) to a peaceful, non-cooperative mass struggle.
This session's reforms elevated the INC's political standing, shaping a new era in the freedom struggle's history. The Congress's commitment to non-cooperation and its emphasis on unity set the stage for transformative actions in India's fight for independence.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which one of the following had proposed a three-tier polity for India?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following had proposed a three-tier polity for India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The **Cabinet Mission** was a group of three British ministers who came to India in **1946** to propose a plan for the transfer of power from the British to the Indian leaders.
The Cabinet Mission proposed a **three-tier polity** for India, consisting of a federal union of India, provincial groupings of states, and provincial governments.
The federal union would have limited powers over defence, foreign affairs, and communications, while the provincial groupings and governments would have autonomy over other subjects.
**

Important Tips
- **Nehru Committee**: The Nehru Committee was a committee of Indian leaders appointed in **1928** to draft a constitution for India.
The Nehru Committee proposed a dominion status for India within the British Commonwealth, with a federal system of government and a bill of rights.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The Tenure of the Congress ministries formed in the elections of 1937 after the Act of 1935 was:
questions.questionText: The Tenure of the Congress ministries formed in the elections of 1937 after the Act of 1935 was:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. On the 22nd of October **1939**, all the Congress ministries applied their resignations after **28 months** of rule in the provinces.
The elections under the Government of India Act, **1935** were held in 1936-37**which led to the formation of Congress ministries. On the issue of World War II, Congress decided to quit the government. **Important Tips**Work Done Under Congress Ministries:**
- Eased curbs on civil liberties-Restrictions on press lifted-Political prisoners and revolutionaries released-Lifted ban from several illegal organisations, books, and journals-Restoration of pensions of officials associated with the Civil Disobedience Movement (CDM)
- Controlled communal riots-Encouragement given to indigenous enterprises-Measures for welfare of Harijans taken"temple entry, use of public facilities, scholarships, an increase in their numbers in government service and police, etc-Police powers were curbed

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which one of the following is NOT true about the First Round Table Conference?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is NOT true about the First Round Table Conference?

options.b: It was to discuss the Report of the Simon Commission
options.d: It was attended by the Congress delegation

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The First Round Table Conference was held in London from November 12, **1930** to January 19, **1931**. The conference was to discuss the Report of the Simon Commission, but the **Indian National Congress boycotted the conference**.
The Congress attended the second Round Table Conference, which took place from 7 September **1931** to 1 December **1931** in London. Mahatma Gandhi represented Indian National Congress in the Second Round Table Conference.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Consider the following statements:
Assertion (A): The Congress boycotted the Simon Commission.
Reason (R): The Simon Commission did not have a single Indian member.
Select the correct answer by using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: Assertion (A): The Congress boycotted the Simon Commission. Reason (R): The Simon Commission did not have a single Indian member. Select the correct answer by using the code given below:

options.a: Both (A) and (R) are true, and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
options.b: Both (A) and (R) are true, and (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The Simon Commission's **lack of Indian representation** directly led to the Congress's decision to **boycott it**.
The absence of Indian members in the commission highlighted the insensitivity of the British government towards involving Indians in shaping their own constitutional future.
This fact was a significant factor in the Congress's decision to protest the commission and its work.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who of the following supported Non-Cooperation Movement, but could not see it's result-
questions.questionText: Who of the following supported Non-Cooperation Movement, but could not see it's result-
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Bal Gangadhar Tilak** was a prominent Indian nationalist and freedom fighter. He was one of the leaders of the Indian independence movement, and he supported the Non-Cooperation Movement. However, he died in **1920**, before the movement could achieve its goals.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Where was Mahatma Gandhi when a raid was carried out by Congress volunteers on Dharsana Salt Depot?
questions.questionText: Where was Mahatma Gandhi when a raid was carried out by Congress volunteers on Dharsana Salt Depot?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Sarojini Naidu, Imam Sahib, and Manilal assumed the responsibility of leading a raid on Dharasana salt works on 21 May **1930**. This event took place while Mahatma Gandhi was confined in **Yerwada Jail**.
**

Important Tips **Dharasana Salt Works in Gujarat:** In May **1930**, the Dharasana Salt Works in Gujarat, India, became a symbol of resistance in the Indian independence struggle led by Mahatma Gandhi and the Indian National Congress.
A peaceful protest against the British salt monopoly took place there, with around 2,500 nonviolent protesters marching to the facility. Faced with brutal beatings by 400 British Indian police officers armed with batons, the satyagrahis did not retaliate, highlighting the cruelty of British rule.
This event garnered global support for Indian independence and prompted negotiations between the British government and the Indian National Congress, advancing the cause of independence.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who represented the Indian National Congress in the Partition Council headed by Lord Mountbatten?
1. Abul Kalam Azad
2. Jawaharlal Nehru
3. Sardar Patel
4. Rajendra Prasad
Select the correct answer from the code given below:
questions.questionText: Who represented the Indian National Congress in the Partition Council headed by Lord Mountbatten? 1. Abul Kalam Azad 2. Jawaharlal Nehru 3. Sardar Patel 4. Rajendra Prasad Select the correct answer from the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Among the given options, **Sardar Patel** and **Rajendra Prasad** represented Indian National Congress in the Partition Council headed by Lord Mountbatten.
Before the establishment of the Partition Council, a Partition Committee was formed. Its members included prominent figures like Vallabh Bhai Patel, Dr. Rajendra Prasad, Liaqat Ali Khan, and Abdur Rab Nishtar.
This committee was later succeeded by the Partition Council, where the Indian National Congress was represented by Sardar Patel, Dr. Rajendra Prasad, and C. Rajgopalachari as an alternate member.
The Muslim League had Mohammed Ali Jinnah, Liaqat Ali Khan, and Abdur Rab Nishtar as alternate members. Following India's independence on August 15, **1947**, the composition of the partition council evolved to include two members from each domain, and during this transitional period, Patel and Dr. Prasad continued to represent the Indian Domain.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The Poorna Swaraj Resolution adopted at the Lahore Session of the Indian National Congress was moved by:
questions.questionText: The Poorna Swaraj Resolution adopted at the Lahore Session of the Indian National Congress was moved by:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The Poorna Swaraj Resolution adopted at the Lahore Session of the Indian National Congress was moved by **Jawaharlal Nehru**.
The resolution, a short 750-word document, called for severing ties with the British and asserting complete independence. It highlighted the economic, political, and cultural injustices caused by British rule in India. Nehru's leadership in this historic declaration marked a significant step towards India's struggle for independence and the subsequent civil disobedience movement.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following called Subhash Chandra Bose as 'Desh Nayak'?
questions.questionText: Who among the following called Subhash Chandra Bose as 'Desh Nayak'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Rabindranath Tagore**, the renowned poet and philosopher, referred to Subhas Chandra Bose as **'Desh Nayak'**, which translates to "National Hero" or "Hero of the Nation".
He even wrote a poem in his honor, titled "Desh Nayak". The poem praises Bose as a "true son of India" and a "hero of the nation". The term reflects the high esteem and respect with which Bose was regarded for his efforts in seeking India's independence from British rule.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: During the freedom struggle, Aruna Asaf Ali was a major woman organizer of underground activities in:
questions.questionText: During the freedom struggle, Aruna Asaf Ali was a major woman organizer of underground activities in:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Aruna Asaf Ali** was a major woman organizer of underground activity during the **Quit India Movement**.
The Quit India Movement, also known as the August Movement, was a mass civil disobedience movement launched by Mahatma Gandhi in **1942**, demanding an end to British colonial rule in India.
She is widely remembered for hoisting the Indian National flag at the Gowalia Tank maidan in Bombay on 9 August **1942**, after most of the Congress leaders were arrested by the British.
She also worked alongside other leaders and activists to coordinate strikes, sabotage, and attacks on government offices and police stations.
She remained underground for several months, evading arrest and inspiring many people to join the movement. She also edited an underground newspaper called Inquilab (Revolution), which reported on the activities and achievements of the movement.
She was awarded the International Lenin Peace Prize in **1964** and the Bharat Ratna, India's highest civilian honour, in **1997** for her contribution to the freedom struggle.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who was the President of Indian National Congress during the visit of Cabinet Mission to India?
questions.questionText: Who was the President of Indian National Congress during the visit of Cabinet Mission to India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The Cabinet Mission was a delegation sent by the British government to India in **1946** to discuss the transfer of power from the British government to the Indian political leadership with the aim of preserving India's unity and granting its independence.
It was headed by three British cabinet members: Pethick Lawrence, Stafford Cripps, and A.V. Alexander. The Viceroy of India, Lord Wavell, also participated in some of the discussions.
President of Indian National Congress during the visit of Cabinet Mission to India was **Maulana Abul Kalam Azad**. He was the president of the Congress from **1940** to **1946**, and he advocated for a united India with provincial autonomy.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Muslim League observed the 'Direct Action Day' on:
questions.questionText: Muslim League observed the 'Direct Action Day' on:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Muslim League observed the **'Direct Action Day'** on **16th August, 1946**. It was the decision to call for 'direct action' to seek a separate Muslim homeland as the British prepared to leave India.
This day is also known as the **1946** Calcutta Killings/**1946** Calcutta Riots, characterized by nationwide communal violence for four days.
It resulted in 5,000 to 10,000 dead, and some 15,000 wounded.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Jiatrang Movement started in:
questions.questionText: Jiatrang Movement started in:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. In **Manipur**, the **Jiatarang movement** began in **1930** with Naga Woman Rani Gaidinliu as its leader.
This movement eventually transformed into a political effort aimed at removing the British from both Manipur and the nearby Naga regions. **

Important Tips **Rani Gaidinliu** ("The Daughter of Hills"):
- Rani Gaidinliu was a spiritual leader among the Naga people.
- She belonged to the Rongmei clan of the Zeliangrong tribe in Manipur's Tamenglong district.
- Born on January 26, **1915**.
- At the age of 13, she joined the efforts of freedom fighter and religious leader, Haipou Jadonang, and became a key figure in his social, religious, and political movement.
- Jadonang, who was also from the Rongmei tribe, initiated the 'Heraka movement,' based on traditional Naga beliefs, with a vision of an independent Naga kingdom (Naga-Raj a).
- After Jadonang's execution, Rani Gaidinliu continued his work, leading the movement which gradually shifted from religious to political focus.
- Rani launched a significant rebellion against the British colonial rule, leading to her imprisonment for life. She was eventually released after 14 years, in **1947**.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following called the Movements of Gandhiji as 'Political Blackmail'?
questions.questionText: Who among the following called the Movements of Gandhiji as 'Political Blackmail'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The British case against Gandhi was based on the Western interpretation of pragmatic justice.
To the British, Gandhi was guilty of calling for a civil-disobedience campaign under Quit India Movement in **1942** which set off a mass outburst.
**Lord Linlithgow** held Gandhi legally responsible for the deaths that had occurred, the damage done. In the Viceroy's words, Gandhi's fast was **"political blackmail"**; as such it was Gandhi's "sole responsibility." This was the official British view.
**

Important Tips
- **Lord Chelmsford** served as Viceroy and Governor General of India from **1916** to **1921**. He succeeded Lord Hardinge as Viceroy of India in **1916**. His time in India began in the aftermath of the British army's defeat in Mesopotamia, and Indian discontent grew as the First World War progressed.
- **Edwin Montagu** was appointed the Secretary of State for India in **1917** and remained in that office till **1922**. On 20th August **1917**, Montagu presented the historic Montagu Declaration (August Declaration) in the British Parliament.
This declaration proposed the increased participation of Indians in the administration and the development of self-governing institutions in India.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The first Trade Union was founded in India in the year 1918 by-
questions.questionText: The first Trade Union was founded in India in the year 1918 by-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. India's first labour union on modern lines was started when workers of Buckingham and Carnatic Mills formed the Madras Labour Union in April **1918**. In **1918**, the first Registered Organized Trade Union was the "Madras Labour Union" founded by **BP Wadia** and V. Kalyanasundaram Mudaliar.
This landmark event is documented meticulously by D. Veeraraghavan in his seminal book 'Making of the Madras Working Class'. **

Important Tips **All India Trade Union Congress (AITUC)** in the year **1920**:
- The founding leaders were Lala Lajpat Rai, N M Joshi & Joseph Baptista.
- Other leaders who participated were Netaji Subhash Chandra Bose, V. V. Giri, Sarojini Naidu, C.R. Das, Jawarharlal Nehru, Mohammad Ali Jinnah, Annie Besant, V.J. Patel, B.P. Wadia, Joseph Baptista, Lalubhai Samaldas, Jamnadas, Dwarka Das, B W Wadia, R R Karandikar, Col. J.C. Wedgwood.
- The AITUC was formed to secure a nominating body for representing Indian Labour at the International Labour Conference.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who formed the Radical Democratic Party in 1940?
questions.questionText: Who formed the Radical Democratic Party in 1940?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. In the year **1940**, the **Radical Democratic Party** was founded by **Manendra Nath Roy**.
It was developed from the League of Radical Congressmen which was founded in **1939** by M.N. Roy. Its main objective was to support the allies in World War II. In the year **1948**, it was dissolved.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who became the President of the Indian National Congress after the resignation of Subhash Chandra Bose?
questions.questionText: Who became the President of the Indian National Congress after the resignation of Subhash Chandra Bose?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Rajendra Prasad** appointed as the president of INC, soon after Subash Chandra Bose resigned in **1939** in Tripuri Session.
Subhas Chandra Bose was elected the President of Tripuri Session. But Gandhi was not happy with S.C. Bose's candidature. At the end S.C. Bose had to resign due to getting lack of support even from leftists.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who of the following presided over the Haripura session of the Indian National Congress?
questions.questionText: Who of the following presided over the Haripura session of the Indian National Congress?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The Haripura session (in Gujarat) of Indian National Congress (INC) held in the year **1938**. It was presided over by **Subhash Chandra Bose**. It was the 51st session of the INC. Haripura session adopted a resolution that the Congress would give moral support to those who were agitating against the governance in the princely states.
**

Important Tips **Subhash Chandra Bose:**
- In **1919**, he had cleared the Indian Civil Services (ICS) examination. Bose, however, resigned later.
- He was highly influenced by Vivekananda's teachings and considered him as his spiritual Guru. His political mentor was Chittaranjan Das.
- Netaji was the editor-in-chief of the newspaper named 'Forward'.
- He even managed to open his own newspaper called 'Swaraj'.
- He established the National Planning Committee in October **1938**. Its main objective was to focus on the development of industries in India.
- S.C. Bose and his followers formed the Forward Bloc in **1939** as a new party within the Congress.
- He reached Japanese-controlled Singapore from Germany in July **1943**, issued from there his famous call, 'Delhi Chalo', and announced the formation of the Azad Hind Government and the Indian National Army on 21st October **1943**.
- His Jayanti is celebrated as 'Parakram Diwas' on 23rd January.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following decided to launch the Independent Party on 16 December 1922?
1. Lala Har Dayal
2. Madan Mohan Malviya
3. Mohammed Ali Jinnah
4. Moti Lal Nehru
Select the correct answer from the codes given below:
questions.questionText: Who among the following decided to launch the Independent Party on 16 December 1922? 1. Lala Har Dayal 2. Madan Mohan Malviya 3. Mohammed Ali Jinnah 4. Moti Lal Nehru Select the correct answer from the codes given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Madan Mohan Malviya** and **Motilal Nehru** made the decision to launch the Independent Party, also known as the Congress-Khilafat Swaraj Party, on 16 December **1922**. This decision stemmed from disagreements that arose following the suspension of the non-cooperation movement in **1922**. The party's objective was to engage in electoral politics and secure representation in legislative bodies to effectively challenge the policies of the British government.
Inspired by the concept of Swaraj, which signifies "independence" or "self-rule," the Swaraj Party aimed to attain greater self-governance and political freedom for the Indian people under British rule.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which of the following institutions were founded during the Non-cooperation Movement (1920-22)?
1. Kashi Vidyapeeth
2. Gujarat Vidyapeeth
3. Jamia Millia
4. Kashi Hindu Vishva Vidyalaya
Select the correct answer from the codes given below.
questions.questionText: Which of the following institutions were founded during the Non-cooperation Movement (1920-22)? 1. Kashi Vidyapeeth 2. Gujarat Vidyapeeth 3. Jamia Millia 4. Kashi Hindu Vishva Vidyalaya Select the correct answer from the codes given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Kashi Hindu Vishva Vidyalaya was formed in **1916**. During the Non-cooperation Movement (1920-22**), the institutions founded were: -**Kashi Vidyapith in 1921**-**Gujarat Vidyapith in 1920**-**Jamia Millia Islamia in 1920 **Important Tips**Kashi Vidyapith:**- Babu Shiv Prasad**Gupta**and Bhagwan Das established Kashi Vidyapith in Varanasi, on 10 February**1921**, during the non-cooperation movement of the freedom struggle. Originally named Kashi Vidyapith, the university was renamed Mahatma Gandhi Kashi Vidyapith in**1995**. It was inaugurated by Mahatma Gandhi. Inspired by Gandhi's principles, it was a hub of the Indian National Movement, fostering self-reliance and attracting notable nationalist scholars.**Gujarat Vidyapith:**- Gujarat Vidyapith, a deemed university in Ahmedabad, India, was founded by Mahatma Gandhi in**1920**to establish Indian-run educational institutions free from British control. It became a deemed university in**1963**. While modernized, it upholds Gandhian principles, emphasizing human studies, social service, and development work. It played a pivotal role in promoting Indian education during the struggle for independence.**Jamia Millia Islamia:**- Jamia Millia Islamia, established in**1920**in Aligarh during British rule, is now a central university in New Delhi. Founded by notable figures like Mahmud Hasan Deobandi, Mohammad Ali Jauhar, Hakim Ajmal Khan, and others, its foundation stone was laid by Mahmud Hasan Deobandi. It gained central university status in**1988**, and its current location is Okhla.**Kashi Hindu Vishva Vidyalaya:**- Banaras Hindu University (BHU), also known as kashi hindu visvavidyalaya, established in**1916** in Varanasi, Uttar Pradesh, India, is a collegiate, central, and research university.
It was formed by incorporating the Central Hindu College founded by Annie Besant in 1898. Madan Mohan Malaviya established BHU with financial support from Maharaja Rameshwar Singh of Dharbhanga, Maharaja Prabhu Narayan Singh of Benares, and lawyer Sunder Lal.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Consider the following statements:
Assertion (A): The Poona Pact defeated the purpose of Communal Award.
Reason (R): It paved the way for reservation of seats in the Parliament and the State Assemblies for the SC and ST people.
Select the correct answer from the code given below:
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: Assertion (A): The Poona Pact defeated the purpose of Communal Award. Reason (R): It paved the way for reservation of seats in the Parliament and the State Assemblies for the SC and ST people. Select the correct answer from the code given below:

options.a: Both (A) and (R) are true, and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
options.b: Both (A) and (R) are true, but (R) is not a correct explanation of (A).

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Assertion (A) is true-The **Poona Pact**, established in **1932**, marked an accord between Mahatma Gandhi and Dr. Babasaheb Ambedkar, representing the Dalits, depressed classes, and upper caste Hindu leaders.
This pact revolved around the reservation of electoral seats for the depressed classes within the British India legislature. It **defeated the purpose of Communal Award** which intended the separate electorates and reserved seats for minorities and depressed classes.
Reason (R) is true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
- The Poona Pact paved the way in facilitating the participation of Dalits in the political arena and contributed to diminishing the inequalities they encountered.
It discarded separate electorates for the Depressed Classes and instead provided for a system of joint electorates with reserved seats. The pact increased the number of reserved seats for the depressed classes (SC and ST) in the Parliament and legislature to 147.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which of the following statements are true about the Simon Commission?
1. It was appointed to enquire into the working of the 1919 Act.
2. It was headed by Sir John Simon.
3. It recommended a Federal Form of Government.
4. It was opposed by the Indian leaders
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements are true about the Simon Commission? 1. It was appointed to enquire into the working of the 1919 Act. 2. It was headed by Sir John Simon. 3. It recommended a Federal Form of Government. 4. It was opposed by the Indian leaders Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct: The Simon Commission, formally known as the Indian Statutory Commission, was indeed appointed to review and enquire into the working of the**Government of India Act of 1919**. The purpose was to assess the reforms that had been implemented and to make recommendations for potential further reforms.**

**Statement 2 is correct: It was chaired by**Sir John Simon**.** He was a British politician and lawyer who led the commission during its study of the constitutional framework in India.
**Statement 3 is correct: Simon Commission advocated for a**Federation-style structure**at the centre, with a 'Council of Greater India' representing the interests of both British India and the princely states.**

**Statement 4 is correct: Yes, the Simon Commission faced**significant opposition**from Indian leaders and political parties.** The main reasons for opposition included the fact that the commission did not have any Indian members and its recommendations were seen as inadequate in addressing Indian demands for self-governance and representation.
**

Important Tips **Recommendations of Simon Commission:**
- Abolish Provincial Dyarchy: Expand ministers' responsibilities to provincial legislatures, eliminating the system of dual control.
- Governor's Special Powers: Grant the Governor special powers to protect minorities and safeguard provinces.
- Representation based on Population: Advocate representation in the Federal Assembly based on population.
- Burma's Dominion Status: Grant Burma Dominion Status and its own Constitution.
- Council of State Representation: Propose indirect election of Council of State members through Provincial Councils, akin to modern Proportional Representation.
- Federation-style Structure: Suggest a 'Council of Greater India' to represent British India and princely states.
- Reservations for Poorest Classes: Recommend reserved seats for the economically disadvantaged.
- Discretionary Powers for Communal Harmony: Empower Governors with discretionary powers to prevent communal tensions.
- Increase in Legislative Council Members: Propose an increase in the number of members in Legislative Councils.
- Local Representation for Frontier Provinces: Establish local legislatures and representation rights for northwestern frontier provinces and Balochistan.
- Administrative Divisions: Divide Sindh from Bombay and Burma from India.
- Indianization of Army: Call for the Indianization of the Indian army.
- Incorporation into Government of India Act **1935**: Integrate the Commission's recommendations into the **1935** Act.
- Government Control over High Courts: Advocate full governmental control over High Courts.

It represented a significant turning point in the struggle for independence. The movement was the most widespread and intense phase of the freedom struggle, as it mobilized millions of people from all sections of society and regions of the country.
It also challenged the British authority and legitimacy like never before, as it witnessed various forms of resistance, such as strikes, boycotts, sabotage, attacks on government offices and police stations, and formation of parallel governments.

This eventually led to discussions and negotiations regarding the transfer of power, culminating in India's independence in **1947**.

- - **1931**), an Indian revolutionary from Maharashtra, was a key figure in the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA).
Notably, be took part in the assassination of British police officer John Saunders. On **March 23, 1931**, he was executed by the British government, alongside Bhagat Singh and Sukhdev Thapar, for their anti-colonial activities.

Important Tips The Lahore Session of the Indian National Congress in **1929** marked a turning point in India's fight for freedom. The session, under President Jawaharlal Nehru, declared complete independence (Puma Swaraj) as the goal. It rejected the Simon Commission, authorized civil disobedience and tax non-payment, and fixed January 26, **1930**, as the first Independence Day.
The Non-cooperation Movement was launched on 5th September **1920** by the Indian National Congress (INC) under the leadership of Mahatma Gandhi.
In September **1920**, in Congress session in Calcutta, the party introduced the Non-Cooperation programme. The Rowlatt Satyagraha, led by Gandhi on April 6, **1919**, protested against the repressive Rowlatt Act passed by the British government.
This Act allowed arrests without trial, indefinite detention, and curbed civil liberties. The protest involved a nationwide hartal (strike), but violent clashes occurred, particularly in Punjab.
The infamous Jallianwala Bagh massacre occurred during these protests, leading to a heavy loss of life. The Act and other laws were repealed in March **1922**.

questions.questionText: Given below is a list of places. Name those where parallel Governments were established during 'Quit India Movement.' Select your answer from the code given below the list: 1. Balia 2. Satara 3. Hazaribagh 4. Meerut 5. Agra Code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Parallel governments were established in **Balia** and **Satara** during the Quit India Movement.
The Quit India Movement, also known as the August Movement, was a mass civil disobedience campaign launched by Mahatma Gandhi and the Indian National Congress in **1942**, demanding an end to British colonial rule in India.
**

Important Tips **Parrallel Governments in**1942**:**
- Four major parallel governments were established in Balliya (Uttar Pradesh), Tamluk (Bengal), Satara (Maharashtra) and Talcher (Orissa).
- In Balia, a district in Uttar Pradesh, a parallel government was formed on 19 August **1942** by Chittu Pandey, a freedom fighter and a Congress leader.
He declared Balia as independent and appointed his own cabinet of ministers. He also issued his own currency and stamps, collected taxes, ran courts, and maintained law and order.
He also organized a volunteer army to resist the British forces. The parallel government lasted for about a month, until the British army recaptured Balia on 23 September **1942**.
- In Satara, a district in Maharashtra, a parallel government was formed on 10 September **1942** by Nana Patil, another freedom fighter and a Congress leader.
He was inspired by the example of Balia and decided to replicate it in Satara. He declared Satara as independent and appointed his own cabinet of ministers.
- In Tamluk in Midnapur district, parallel government, known as Jatiya Sarkar, was established on December 17, **1942**. It lasted until September **1944**.
- In Talcher in Orissa, parallel government was established in **1943** by some local Congress workers. They took control of the police station and declared independence from British rule.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: In the meeting of All India Committee on June 14-15, 1947; who among voted against the partition of India?
questions.questionText: In the meeting of All India Committee on June 14-15, 1947; who among voted against the partition of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. In the meetings of All India Committee on June 14-15, **1947**, **Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan** voted against the partititon of India.
He aligned himself with the pro-unity stance of the Indian National Congress and All-India Azad Muslim Conference, rather than the pro-partition All-India Muslim League.
However, his trust in the Indian National Congress was shattered when they reluctantly accepted the partition plan without consulting the Khudai Khidmatgar leaders, leading him to express his disappointment by stating, "you have thrown us to the wolves." In June **1947**, Khan, along with other Khudai Khidmatgar leaders, formally presented the Bannu Resolution to the British authorities, demanding the creation of an independent state of Pashtunistan encompassing all Pashtun territories of British India, separate from the emerging state of Pakistan.
Unfortunately, the British government openly rejected the resolution's demands, and Khan's vision of Pashtunistan as a separate, independent entity was not realized.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which Indian revolutionary helped Subhash Chandra Bose in the establishment of Azad Hind Fauz?
questions.questionText: Which Indian revolutionary helped Subhash Chandra Bose in the establishment of Azad Hind Fauz?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Ras Bihari Bose**, an Indian revolutionary, played a significant role in assisting Subhas Chandra Bose in the establishment of the Azad Hind Fauz (Indian National Army). Bose arrived in Singapore in **1943** and was invited by Ras Bihari Bose to take over the leadership of the Indian Independence League in East Asia.
Ras Bihari Bose's efforts and connections were instrumental in providing the foundation and support for the formation of the Azad Hind Fauz, which aimed to fight for India's independence from British rule.
**

Important Tips Some other Indian revolutionaries who helped Subhas Chandra Bose in the establishment of the Azad Hind Fauj:
- **Mohan Singh**: Mohan Singh was the first commander of the Azad Hind Fauj. He was captured by the British in **1942** and was imprisoned for several years.
- **Captain Lakshmi Sahgal**: Lakshmi Sahgal was a doctor who joined the Azad Hind Fauj in **1943**. She was known as the "Joan of Arc of India" for her bravery and leadership.
- **Captain Shah Nawaz Khan**: Shah Nawaz Khan was a Muslim officer who joined the Azad Hind Fauj in **1943**. He was captured by the British and was tried for treason.
He was acquitted of all charges in **1946**.

**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The Kanpur Bolshevik conspiracy case was a controversial court case initiated in British India in **1924**. Kanpur Conspiracy Case was also against the **communists** which were abhorred by the British Government.
Some newly turned communists named M N Roy, Muzaffar Ahamed, S A Dange, Shaukat Usmani, Nalini **Gupta**, Singaravelu Chettiar, Ghulam Hussain were caught by the Government and were trailed for conspiring against the Government.
The Charge on them was "to deprive the King-Emperor of his sovereignty of British India, by complete separation of India from imperialistic Britain by a violent revolution."

- **Chauri-Chaura incident** (February 5, **1922**)
- **Suspension of Non-Cooperation Movement** (February 12, **1922**)
- **Bardoli Resolutions** (**1928**) **

Important Tips
- **Chauri-Chaura incident** (February 5, **1922**): The Chauri Chaura incident was a violent protest that took place in the town of Chauri Chaura in Uttar Pradesh.
A group of protesters clashed with police and set fire to a police station, killing 22 policemen.
- **Suspension of Non-Cooperation Movement** (February 12, **1922**): The Chauri Chaura incident hocked Mahatma Gandhi, who felt that the Non cooperation movement had become violent and that it was no longer following the principles of non-violence.
He called off the movement on February 12, **1922**.
- **Bardoli Resolutions** (**1928**): The Bardoli Resolution, drafted in **1928**, was a pivotal document during the Bardoli Satyagraha.
It demanded the withdrawal of unfair tax hikes and fair treatment for farmers. The resolution's impact extended to Saran, Bihar, inspiring locals to challenge oppressive British policies.
Its courage and ideals continued to influence Saran's involvement in the struggle for freedom and justice.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who of the following persons called Irwin and Gandhi 'The Two Mahatmas'?
questions.questionText: Who of the following persons called Irwin and Gandhi 'The Two Mahatmas'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Sarojini Naidu** referred to Mahatma Gandhi and Lord Irwin as "the two Mahatmas" due to their central roles in the events leading up to the Second Round Table Conference in **1931** and the Salt Satyagraha of **1930**. Gandhi's leadership of the Indian National Congress prompted the influential Salt Satyagraha against the unjust salt law, resulting in his imprisonment and global attention, Lord Irwin, the Viceroy, sought negotiations and released Gandhi, leading to a series of eight meetings that culminated in the Gandhi-Irwin Pact.
This recognition by Naidu highlights their significance in striving for India's independence through non-violent means and peaceful dialogue amid British imperialism.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which were not allocated separate electorate and reserved seats by Communal Award of MacDonald?
questions.questionText: Which were not allocated separate electorate and reserved seats by Communal Award of MacDonald?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The Communal Award gave separate electorates and reserved seats to Muslims, Sikhs, and Depressed Castes.
However, it did not give separate electorates or reserved seats to **Buddhists**. The reason for this was that the number of Buddhists in British India was relatively small.
In addition, the Buddhists were not seen as a distinct religious community by the British.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The American publicist who was with Mahatma Gandhi during his Quit India movement was:
questions.questionText: The American publicist who was with Mahatma Gandhi during his Quit India movement was:

**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Louis Fischer** was an American journalist and author who wrote extensively about world politics and history.
He was a close friend and admirer of Mahatma Gandhi, and he accompanied him during the **Quit India Movement of 1942**. He also wrote a biography of Gandhi, titled The Life of Mahatma Gandhi, which was published in **1950** and later adapted into the Oscar-winning film Gandhi in **1982**.
**

Important Tips
- **William L. Shirer**: He was an American journalist and historian who reported on the rise of Nazi Germany and World War II.
He is best known for his book The Rise and Fall of the Third Reich, which is a comprehensive history of Nazi Germany based on his personal observations and documents.
- **Web Miller**: He was an American reporter who covered several major events in the 20th century, such as the Mexican Revolution, the Irish War of Independence, the Spanish Civil War, and World War II.
He also interviewed several prominent figures, such as Adolf Hitler, Benito Mussolini, Franklin D. Roosevelt, and Jawaharlal Nehru.
- **Negley Farson**: He was an American adventurer, journalist, and author who traveled extensively around the world and wrote about his experiences.
He covered i various conflicts and revolutions, such as the Russian I Revolution, the Chinese Civil War, the Spanish Civil War, and World War II. He also met several influential leaders, such as Winston Churchill, Joseph Stalin, Mao Zedong, and Chiang Kai-shek.

**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The **Civil Disobedience Movement** started with well-known Dandi March. The Dandi March was a 241-mile (385 km) protest march undertaken by Mahatma Gandhi and his followers in **1930** to protest against the British salt monopoly.

The resolution, which gained unanimous approval from the Subject Committee, endorsed the idea of a united homeland for Muslims and proposed the formation of an independent Muslim state.
During the general session, A. K. Fazlul Huq, the chief minister of undivided Bengal, introduced the resolution, supported by leaders like **Chaudhry Khaliquzzaman** from the United Provinces, Zafar Ali Khan from Punjab, Sardar Aurangzeb Khan from North-West Frontier Province, and Sir Abdullah Haroon from Sindh.
Qazi Muhammad Essa from Balochistan and other leaders also expressed their support.

statementLines: Who among the following gave up his legal practice during the Non-Cooperation Movement?
questions.questionText: Who among the following gave up his legal practice during the Non-Cooperation Movement?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. During the **Non-Cooperation Movement**, **Chittaranjan Das** (also known as Deshbandhu) gave up his legal practice.
He was a prominent Indian freedom fighter, political activist, and lawyer who played a vital role in the Indian Independence Movement. He suspended his legal practice to actively participate in the movement against British colonial rule. Chittaranjan Das was a key figure in advocating for non-cooperation with the British government and promoting the use of indigenous goods, including Khadi clothing.

statementLines: Who among the following issued the Communal Award?
questions.questionText: Who among the following issued the Communal Award?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The **Communal Award** was a plan for the political representation of different religious communities in British India.
It was announced by the British Prime Minister **Ramsay Macdonald** on **August 16, 1932**. The Communal Award was based on the principle of separate electorates, which meant that different religious communities would have their own electorates to elect their representatives to the legislature.
This was done to protect the interests of minority communities, such as Muslims and Sikhs.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Who among the following were associated with the formation of Swaraj Party?
1. Subhash Chandra Bose
2. C.R. Das
3. Jawaharlal Nehru
4. Motilal Nehru
Select the correct answer from the codes given below:
questions.questionText: Who among the following were associated with the formation of Swaraj Party? 1. Subhash Chandra Bose 2. C.R. Das 3. Jawaharlal Nehru 4. Motilal Nehru Select the correct answer from the codes given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The **Swaraj Party**, also known as the Congress-Khilafat Swaraj Party, was formed by **Chittaranjan Das and Motilal Nehru** after the Gaya annual conference of the Indian National Congress in December **1922**. Its primary goal was to advocate for greater self-governance and political freedom from British rule.
Subhash Chandra Bose and Jawaharlal Nehru were not directly associated with the formation of the Swaraj Party. **

Important Tips Background of formation of Swaraj Party:
The suspension of the non-cooperation movement in **1922** brought forth significant disagreements within the Congress Partys leadership. A divide emerged among the pioneers, with some advocating for the continuation of non-cooperation while others aimed to conclude the boycott of legislatures and engage in electoral contests.
The two factions were termed no-changers and pro-changers. The former group championed the persistence of non-cooperation, while the latter sought to transition towards participating in elections and legislative bodies.
During the Gaya session of the Congress in **1922**, C R Das, who was presiding over the session, proposed the idea of entering the legislatures.
However, this proposition was defeated, revealing the contrasting viewpoints within the party. Consequently, C R Das and fellow leaders who supported participation in legislatures opted to break away from the Congress.
They established the Congress-Khilafat Swarajaya Party, naming Das as its president and enlisting Nehru as one of the secretaries.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following ran Congress Radio during the Quit-India movement:
questions.questionText: Who among the following ran Congress Radio during the Quit-India movement:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Usha Mehta** was a freedom fighter who was known for her role in setting up the **Congress Radio**, an underground radio station that operated for about three months during the **Quit India Movement of 1942**. Congress Radio was the broadcasting mouthpiece of the Indian National Congress and functioned from different locations in Bombay, present-day Mumbai, and briefly from Nashik.
Congress Radio was organized by Usha Mehta, then a 22-year-old student activist, with the help of amateur radio operators. The radio station broadcast messages related to the Indian independence movement and recorded speeches from some of the prominent leaders of the movement.
The radio station broadcast from 27 August through 12 November **1942** before being shut down by the authorities, with the operators being arrested.
**

Important Tips
- **Jai Prakash Narayan**: He led the opposition against Prime Minister Indira Gandhi in the mid-1970s, and he called for a total revolution to overthrow her.
He was also involved in the Quit India Movement in **1942**, and he escaped from the Hazaribagh Central Jail along with some other freedom fighters.

statementLines: Which one of the following was the result of the Khilafat movement?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following was the result of the Khilafat movement?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The **Khilafat Movement** had important consequences for India. The Khilafat Movement **united Hindus and Muslims**.
Before this movement, Hindus and Muslims did not cooperate much with each other. Hindus supported the Muslims in this movement. This helped reduce communal tensions for some time.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who was the President of the Indian National Congress when it passed the Quit India resolution?
questions.questionText: Who was the President of the Indian National Congress when it passed the Quit India resolution?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Maulana Abul Kalam Azad** was the president of the Indian National Congress when it passed the **Quit India resolution on 8 August 1942** in Bombay.
He was also one of the senior leaders who were arrested by the British authorities on the night of 9 August **1942**. He remained in prison until **1945**.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The formula for transfer of sovereignty to India in 1947 was known as-
questions.questionText: The formula for transfer of sovereignty to India in 1947 was known as-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The **Mountbatten Plan** was a plan for the transfer of power from British rule to Indian rule in **1947**. It was announced by **Lord Mountbatten**, the last Viceroy of India, on **June 3, 1947**. The plan called for the partition of British India into two dominions, a Hindu-majority India and a Muslim-majority Pakistan.
The Mountbatten Plan was accepted by the Indian National Congress and the Muslim League, and it was approved by the British Parliament. The plan came into effect on August 15, **1947**, when India and Pakistan became independent dominions. **

Important Tips The Mountbatten Plans Provisions were as follows:
- British India would be divided into two dominions: India and Pakistan.
- The constitution drafted by the Constituent Assembly would not be applicable to Muslim-majority areas, as they would become part of Pakistan.
- According to the plan, the legislative assemblies of Bengal and Punjab convened and voted in favor of partition.
- The legislative assembly of Sind would make the choice of joining the Indian constituent assembly or not.
It opted to join Pakistan.
- A referendum was scheduled for NWFP (North Western Frontier Province) to determine its dominion affiliation.
NWFP chose to join Pakistan, although Khan Abdul Gaffar Khan boycotted and rejected the referendum.
- The transfer of power was set for August 15, **1947**.
- To delineate the international boundaries between the two nations, the Boundary Commission was established, with Sir Cyril Radcliffe serving as its chairperson.
The commission was tasked with demarcating Bengal and Punjab into the two new countries.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Who was elected as President of the All-India Khilafat Conference in 1919?
questions.questionText: Who was elected as President of the All-India Khilafat Conference in 1919?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Mahatma Gandhi** was elected as President of the **All-India Khilafat Conference** (23rd November **1919**).
Khilafat Committee was formed in Bombay On 20 March **1919**. An all-India Khilafat Conference was organized in Delhi on 23 November **1919**. Mahatma Gandhi saw it as an opportunity to bring together Hindus and Muslims on a common platform for the nationalist movement.
For the upcoming Non-cooperation Movement, the leaders of the Khilafat Movement joined hands with Indian National Congress.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Motilal Nehru was the leader of Swaraj Party. Who of the following was NOT in the Party?
questions.questionText: Motilal Nehru was the leader of Swaraj Party. Who of the following was NOT in the Party?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Chakravarti Rajagopalachari**, commonly known as C.R. Rajaji, was **not a member of the Swaraj Party**.
He was member of the Indian National Congress and later founded the Swatantra Party in **1959**. Rajagopalachari played a prominent role in the Indian freedom movement, serving as a leader of the Congress, Premier of the Madras Presidency, Governor-General of India, and Chief Minister of Madras state.
Srinivas Iyer, Chittaranjan Das, and Vithalbhai Patel were ali members of the Swaraj Party. **

Important Tips
- **Srinivasa Iyengar (1874 -**1941**)** was an Indian lawyer, freedom fighter, and politician linked with the Indian National Congress.
He served as Advocate-General of Madras, was a law member of the Governors executive council, and broke away with leaders like Motilal Nehru and Chittaranjan Das to form the Swarajya Party in **1923**. He mentored prominent leaders like K. Kamaraj.
His book Maynes Hindu Laws is well-regarded. Despite his accomplishments, he faced criticism for the Congresss Brahmin-dominated image during his leadership.
- **Chittaranjan Das (1870 -**1925**)**, known as Deshbandhu, was an Indian freedom fighter, political leader, and lawyer in the Indian Independence Movement.
He founded the Swaraj Party in undivided Bengal and mentored Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose. He defended Aurobindo Ghosh in the Alipore bomb case. He played a key role in the Non-Cooperation Movement, promoted Hindu-Muslim unity, and advocated national education.
He founded the Congress-Khilafat Swaraj Party and was the first mayor of Calcutta.
- **Vithalbhai Patel (1873 -**1933**)**, co-founder of the Swaraj Party and elder brother of Sardar Patel, was an influential Indian legislator.
He joined the Congress but differed from Gandhis leadership. He played a key role in the Swaraj Party, aiming to sabotage the government through councils.
As President of Indias Central Legislative Assembly, he established procedural practices and neutrality conventions in debates.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Among the following whose adjournment was proposed in Gandhi-Irwin Pact?
questions.questionText: Among the following whose adjournment was proposed in Gandhi-Irwin Pact?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The **Gandhi-Irwin Pact** was an agreement between Mahatma Gandhi and the British Viceroy, Lord Irwin, to end the Civil Disobedience Movement.
The pact was signed on **5 March 1931**. The Pact proposed the **adjournment of the Civil Disobedience Movement** in exchange for the British governments agreement to release political prisoners, withdraw ordinances, and allow the Indian National Congress to participate in the Second Round Table Conference.
**

Important Tips Features of Gandhi-Irwin Pact:
- The Indian National Congress (INC) decided to join the Round Table Conference.
- The INC pledged to halt the civil disobedience movement.
- The withdrawal of all laws that restricted the Congress activities.
- Discontinuation of legal actions except for those related to violent offenses.
- Liberation of individuals imprisoned for participating in civil disobedience.
- Elimination of the tax on salt. Outcomes of the Gandhi-Irwin Pact:
- The INC actively participated in the Second Round Table Conference held between September and December of **1931**.
- The government committed to repealing all enacted ordinances.
- It agreed to release political detainees except those involved in violent acts.
- Peaceful picketing of liquor and foreign cloth shops was permitted.
- The prohibition on the INCs activities was lifted.
- The properties confiscated from Satyagrahis were returned.
- People living near coastal areas were allowed to gather salt.
- Outstanding fines were waived.
- Government employees who had resigned during the civil disobedience movement were treated leniently.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: What was the policy of Indian National Congress during 2nd World War?
questions.questionText: What was the policy of Indian National Congress during 2nd World War?

options.a: Cooperation of Britain in the case of assurance of Complete Independence

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The policy of Indian National Congress during 2nd World War was that the country was prepared to **cooperate with the British for World War II, but on the condition that there be an immediate declaration by Britain, granting India complete independence**.
It also demanded the establishment of a national government. Indian National Congress (INC) initially decided to support the British war effort in World War II for a number of reasons.
The INC was concerned about the threat posed by Nazi Germany and its allies. The INC also believed that it was in Indias strategic interest to support the British war effort.
The INC argued that if the British were defeated, India would be vulnerable to attack from Japan. The INC was also hoping that by supporting the British war effort, it would be able to pressure the British government to grant India independence after the war.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Simon Commission in 1928 came to India with the purpose-
questions.questionText: Simon Commission in 1928 came to India with the purpose-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The **Simon Commission**, formally known as the Indian Statutory Commission, was sent to India in **1928**. Its primary purpose was to **study and recommend potential administrative reforms** for the British possession in India.
The commission was chaired by Sir John Simon and consisted of seven members from the British Parliament. Although it aimed to assess constitutional reform, it faced significant criticism for its lack of Indian representation and led to increased demands for Indian self-governance.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who gave the slogan Jai Hind?
questions.questionText: Who gave the slogan Jai Hind?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The slogan **Jai Hind** was given by **Subhas Chandra Bose**. Jai Hind became the rallying cry for the INA and a symbol of patriotic fervor.
It was used to inspire and motivate the soldiers of the INA as well as the general Indian population to unite against British rule. The slogan encapsulated the spirit of Indias struggle for freedom and conveyed a strong sense of nationalism and pride.

statementLines: Consider the following statements;
1. The growth of socialist tendency in the national movement led to the foundation of the Congress Socialist party in 1934 under the leadership of Acharya Narendra Deva and Jayaprakash Narayan.
2. In 1936, Jawaharlal Nehru urged the Congress to accept socialism as its goal and to bring itself closer to the peasantry and the working class.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements; 1. The growth of socialist tendency in the national movement led to the foundation of the Congress Socialist party in 1934 under the leadership of Acharya Narendra Deva and Jayaprakash Narayan. 2. In 1936, Jawaharlal Nehru urged the Congress to accept socialism as its goal and to bring itself closer to the peasantry and the working class. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct-The growth of socialist tendency in the national movement led to the**foundation of the Congress Socialist Party in 1934**under the leadership of**Acharya Narendra Dev, Ram Manohar Lohia, and Jayaprakash Narayan**.** The party was formed by a group of Congressmen who were dissatisfied with the partys moderate policies. They wanted the Congress to adopt a more radical approach to social and economic reform.
**Statement 2 is correct-In**1936**,**Jawaharlal Nehru**, who was the leader of the Congress Socialist Party,**urged the Congress to accept socialism as its goal**and to bring itself closer to the peasantry and the working class.** Nehrus speech was a major turning point in the history of the Congress. It marked the beginning of the Congresss shift towards a more socialist ideology.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Which one of the following is NOT correct about Mahatma Gandhis Dandi Yatra or Salt Match?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is NOT correct about Mahatma Gandhis Dandi Yatra or Salt Match?

options.a: It was the first national activity in which women participated in large numbers.
options.b: Gandhiji allowed the womens participation at the request of Kamaladevi Chattopadhyay.
options.c: The march was fully covered by the Western press.
options.d: The march began after the failure of the First Round Table Conference in London.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.

Option a is correct
- **Women were an integral part of the Salt March** from the very beginning.
Some of the women who participated in the Salt March are-Sarojini Naidu, Kamala Chattopadhyay, Kasturba Gandhi, Aruna Asaf Ali, and Mahadevi Verma.
Option b is correct-Gandhiji initially did not want women to participate in the Salt March, as he felt that it was too dangerous for them. However, he was eventually persuaded by the request of **Kamaladevi Chattopadhyay**, a leading Indian independence activist and social reformer.
Option c is correct-The march was a major news story, and it was **reported in newspapers and magazines all over the world**. The Western press was fascinated by the nonviolent methods of protest used by Gandhi and his followers, and they saw the Salt March as a symbol of Indias struggle for independence.
Some of the press where it was published includes-New York Times, The Times of London, The Guardian, and The Washington Post.

Option d is incorrect-The First Round Table Conference took place from November **1930** to January **1931**, while the **Dandi March**, initiated by Mahatma Gandhi and his followers, **occurred prior to this conference**.
The Dandi March lasted from March 12, **1930**, to April 6, **1930**, and was a direct action against the campaign of tax resistance and non-violent protest aimed at challenging the British salt monopoly.
It was a significant event in Indias struggle for independence, symbolizing civil disobedience and drawing international attention to the movement.

statementLines: Who among the following were jailed in the Kanpur Bolshevik Conspiracy case in 1924?
questions.questionText: Who among the following were jailed in the Kanpur Bolshevik Conspiracy case in 1924?

options.a: Muzaffar Ahmad, SA Dange. Shaukat Usmani, Nalini Gupta

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Kanpur Bolshevik Conspiracy case** was a court case initiated by the British government in **1924** against some communist activists who were accused of seeking to overthrow the British rule by a violent revolution.
The accused included **Muzaffar Ahmad, SA Dange, Shaukat Usmani, Nalini**Gupta, Singaravelu Chettiar, Ghulam Hussain and MN Roy. MN Roy was charged in absentia, Ghulam Hussain turned an informer and was pardoned, and the rest were sentenced to four years of imprisonment.
The case attracted public attention to the communist ideology and its aims in India.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Surya Sen** was not associated with the Kakori Conspiracy case. The **Kakori Conspiracy case**, also known as the Kakori Train Robbery, was a famous incident that took place in **1925** in Kakori, near Lucknow, India.
The case involved a group of revolutionaries who attempted to rob a train carrying government funds. Surya Sen was a revolutionary leader who led the Chittagong armoury raid in **1930** in Bengal.

The Mountbatten Plan, also known as the June 3rd Plan, was proposed by Lord Mountbatten, the last Viceroy of India, in **1947**. The plan outlined the steps for the partition of British India into two separate dominions, India and Pakistan.
